definition: The $900B-$1.5T sub-investment-grade debt maturing 2025-2028 is a near-arithmetic certainty that will force credit vol repricing, as borrowers face 200-400bp higher all-in refinancing costs representing >40% cash interest expense increase for typical B-rated credits. Covenant-lite documentation (>90% of institutional loans lack maintenance covenants, EBITDA addbacks at 25-40%) eliminates early warning and guarantees nonlinear transition.
evidence: Debt stock and coupon differential are observable facts not forecasts
Refinancing math: B-rated issued at 5.5-7.0% faces 8.5-11.0%, a 40-60% cash interest increase
CLO arbitrage viability is binary: functioning at SOFR ~3.5%, dead at SOFR ~5.5%
Post-reinvestment CLO expirations of $250-350B over 18 months remove marginal bid for 65-70% of institutional leveraged loans
Scenario-weighted default rate 4.3% expected vs. 2.5% market-implied
100-200bps spread widening pushes 15-25% into distressed territory
2019-2020 vintage CLOs exiting reinvestment as maturity wall hits
2015-2016 precedent: 30-40% of secondary market volume from forced selling
Pair_1 debate judged credit_analyst_02 'stronger' on this point
open_questions: What percentage of borrowers can escape via private credit refinancing?
What will the macro environment be at point of maturity wall impact?
Does the timing uncertainty (2-3 year A&E deferrals) change the vol implications?
How much can amend-and-extend activity pre-emptively address the maturity wall?
Is the specific default-rate gap sensitive to subjective probability weights?
What is the current refinancing rate for commodity-sector borrowers?
How much CLO capacity remains for commodity sector reinvestment?
Does the maturity wall timing coincide with or amplify the credit impulse turning negative?
